title: Android Fundamentals
description: Core building blocks of Android applications

references.title: Android Developers — Intents and Intent Filters
Android Developers — Tasks and Back Stack

lesson:
The Android Application Model

Android apps are built from four core components — Activity, Service, BroadcastReceiver, and ContentProvider. Unlike web apps with a single entry point, Android apps have multiple entry points.

ComponentPurposeManaged ByLifetime
ActivitySingle screen with UIActivityManagerServiceUntil finished or system destroys
ServiceBackground work, no UIActiveServices (part of AMS)Until stopped or killed
BroadcastReceiverResponds to system eventsBroadcastQueue (part of AMS)Duration of onReceive() only
ContentProviderShared data interfaceContentProviderManagerWhile in use by clients

Activity Lifecycle

onCreate() → onStart() → onResume() [RUNNING - visible & interactive] onPause() ← user partially obscured (dialog, multi-window) onStop() ← fully hidden (home button, new activity) onDestroy() ← finishing or system reclaiming memory Rotation: onPause → onStop → onDestroy → onCreate → onStart → onResume (entire Activity recreated! ViewModel survives this)

How Intents Work — The System Path

When you call startActivity(intent), the request travels via Binder IPC to ActivityManagerService in the system_server process:

Your App Process system_server Process ───────────────── ──────────────────── startActivity(intent) │ └──► Binder IPC ──────────────► ActivityManagerService (AMS) │ ├── resolveIntent() │ └── PackageManagerService │ └── scans all apps' │ AndroidManifest.xml │ for matching intent-filters ├── Check permissions ├── Find or create target Task ├── Find or create target Process │ └── Zygote forks if needed └── Send to target app │ Target App ◄──────────────────────────────────┘ ActivityThread.handleLaunchActivity() └── activity.onCreate(bundle)
  • Explicit Intent — specifies exact target: Intent(this, DetailActivity::class.java)
  • Implicit Intent — declares an action: Intent(ACTION_SEND). PMS scans all manifests for matching <intent-filter>. Multiple matches → chooser dialog

Tasks vs Activities vs Processes

These are three separate concepts that are commonly confused:

Process (Linux process — one per app, has PID and memory) └── Application (singleton, global state) └── ActivityThread (main/UI thread) Task (logical back stack — can span processes!) └── Activity A (bottom of stack) └── Activity B └── Activity C (top — foreground, visible)
Key insight: A Task is NOT a process. Tasks can contain Activities from different apps/processes. When your app launches the Camera app, the Camera Activity joins YOUR task's back stack.

When Is a New Task Created?

  • User taps app icon → Launcher sends FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK → new Task
  • Code uses FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK
  • Activity has launchMode="singleTask" in manifest
  • Deep link or notification with NEW_TASK flag

What Happens When Activity B Opens an Already-Existing Activity A?

launchModeBehavior
standard (default)New instance of A created in B's Task. Two copies of A now exist.
singleTopIf A is on TOP of current Task → reuse + onNewIntent(). Otherwise → new instance.
singleTaskA's Task comes to foreground. Activities above A are destroyed. A gets onNewIntent().
singleInstanceA is alone in its Task. Brought to front, gets onNewIntent(). No other Activity can join.

Window Management & DecorView

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ SurfaceFlinger │ │ (composites all surfaces into final frame) │ ├──────────┬──────────────────┬────────────────────┤ │ StatusBar│ App Surface │ NavigationBar │ │ (SystemUI│ (DecorView) │ (SystemUI) │ │ window) │ │ │ └──────────┴──────────────────┴────────────────────┘ WindowManagerService (WMS) — in system_server ├── Manages all windows (Z-ordering, bounds) ├── Input dispatching (touch → correct window) └── Window animations ActivityManagerService (AMS) └── Activity lifecycle, tasks, back stack └── Tells WMS to add/remove windows DecorView (in YOUR app process) └── Root View of every Activity window └── PhoneWindow creates it └── Your setContentView() layout goes inside
Multi-window (AAOS/split-screen): SystemUI doesn't modify your DecorView. Instead, WMS assigns bounds to each Task's window, and SystemUI adds separate caption bar windows (via WindowDecorService) positioned at the top of each Task's bounds. The app's window area is simply made smaller.

SurfaceView vs TextureView

SurfaceViewTextureView
SurfacesOwn surface (2 total — punches a hole)Uses app surface (1)
ThreadAny threadAny thread
Alpha/rotationNoYes
PerformanceBest (HW overlay)Good (GPU texture copy)
Use caseCamera preview, video, mapsVideo with alpha/effects

questions.title: The Four Components
questions.description: What are the 4 fundamental building blocks of an Android application?
questions.explanation: The four fundamental Android components are: Activity (UI screens), Service (background operations), BroadcastReceiver (responds to system-wide events), and ContentProvider (manages shared data). Fragments are UI components within Activities, not standalone components. Intents are messaging objects, not components.
hints: Think about what you register in AndroidManifest.xml
Fragments live inside Activities — they are not top-level components

questions.title: Activity Lifecycle
questions.description: Which lifecycle callback is called when an Activity first becomes visible to the user (but may not yet be in the foreground)?
questions.explanation: onCreate() initializes the Activity but it is not yet visible. onStart() is called when the Activity becomes visible. onResume() is called when it moves to the foreground and becomes interactive. onVisible() does not exist in the Android lifecycle.
hints: The lifecycle order is: onCreate → onStart → onResume
Visible and interactive are two different states

questions.title: Creating an Intent
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to navigate from the current Activity to DetailActivity, passing an item ID.
questions.code:
// Navigate to DetailActivity
val intent = ___1___(this, DetailActivity::class.java)
intent.putExtra("ITEM_ID", itemId)
___2___(intent)

blanks.hint: Method to launch the new Activity

questions.explanation: An Intent is created with the current context and target Activity class. startActivity(intent) launches the target Activity. Data is passed via putExtra() key-value pairs.
hints: The class that represents a messaging object in Android
The Activity method that launches another Activity

questions.title: What is an Intent?
questions.description: In your own words, explain what an Intent is in Android and give an example of when you would use one.
questions.answer: An Intent is a messaging object used to request an action from another app component. It can be used to start an Activity, start a Service, or deliver a broadcast. For example, you use an explicit Intent to navigate between screens in your app, or an implicit Intent to open a URL in the browser.
questions.explanation: Intents are the primary mechanism for inter-component communication in Android. Explicit intents specify the exact component to start. Implicit intents declare a general action, letting the system find a suitable component.
hints: Think about how different parts of an Android app communicate
There are two types: explicit and implicit

questions.title: Missing Permission
questions.description: This app crashes with a SecurityException when trying to fetch data from the internet. Fix the AndroidManifest.xml.
questions.starterCode:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android">

    <application
        android:allowBackup="true"
        android:label="@string/app_name"
        android:theme="@style/Theme.MyApp">

        <activity
            android:name=".MainActivity"
            android:exported="true">
            <intent-filter>
                <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />
                <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" />
            </intent-filter>
        </activity>

    </application>

</manifest>
questions.solution:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android">

    <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.INTERNET" />

    <application
        android:allowBackup="true"
        android:label="@string/app_name"
        android:theme="@style/Theme.MyApp">

        <activity
            android:name=".MainActivity"
            android:exported="true">
            <intent-filter>
                <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />
                <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" />
            </intent-filter>
        </activity>

    </application>

</manifest>
questions.explanation: Android requires explicit permission declarations. The <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.INTERNET" /> tag must be added before <application>.
hints: What XML tag declares permissions in AndroidManifest.xml?
The permission should go between <manifest> and <application>

questions.title: AndroidManifest.xml
questions.description: What is the primary purpose of the AndroidManifest.xml file?
options: To define the app's visual theme and styling
To declare app components, permissions, and metadata to the Android system
To configure build settings like minSdk and dependencies
To store string resources for localization
questions.explanation: The AndroidManifest.xml is the app's identity card to the Android system. It declares all components (Activities, Services, etc.), required permissions, hardware features, and app metadata. The system reads it to know what your app contains and what it needs. Build settings go in build.gradle, themes in themes.xml, and strings in strings.xml.
hints: Think about what the system needs to know about your app before launching it

questions.title: Implicit Intent
questions.description: What happens when this code runs?
questions.code:
val sendIntent = Intent().apply {
    action = Intent.ACTION_SEND
    putExtra(Intent.EXTRA_TEXT, "Check out this app!")
    type = "text/plain"
}
val chooser = Intent.createChooser(sendIntent, "Share via")
startActivity(chooser)
options: It opens a specific messaging app and sends the text
It shows a system dialog letting the user choose which app to share text with
It sends an SMS message to all contacts
It crashes because no target Activity is specified
questions.explanation: This creates an implicit intent with ACTION_SEND — it doesn't specify a target app. createChooser() wraps it in a chooser dialog that shows all apps capable of handling text/plain sharing (email, messaging, social media, etc.). The user picks which app to use. This is the standard Android sharing mechanism.
hints: No specific Activity class is mentioned — how does the system know where to send it?

questions.title: Registering a BroadcastReceiver
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to register a BroadcastReceiver that listens for airplane mode changes.

blanks.hint: The base class for broadcast listeners
The callback method when a broadcast arrives

questions.explanation: BroadcastReceiver is the base class. onReceive() is called when a matching broadcast arrives. IntentFilter specifies which broadcasts to listen for. Remember to unregisterReceiver() in onDestroy() to prevent leaks.
hints: The class extends a specific Android component type
The filter tells the system which broadcasts you care about

questions.title: Activity Not in Manifest
questions.description: The app crashes with "ActivityNotFoundException" when navigating to SettingsActivity. The Activity class exists and compiles fine. Fix it.
questions.starterCode:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android">
    <application
        android:label="@string/app_name"
        android:theme="@style/Theme.MyApp">

        <activity
            android:name=".MainActivity"
            android:exported="true">
            <intent-filter>
                <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />
                <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" />
            </intent-filter>
        </activity>

        <!-- SettingsActivity is missing! -->

    </application>
</manifest>
questions.solution:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<manifest xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android">
    <application
        android:label="@string/app_name"
        android:theme="@style/Theme.MyApp">

        <activity
            android:name=".MainActivity"
            android:exported="true">
            <intent-filter>
                <action android:name="android.intent.action.MAIN" />
                <category android:name="android.intent.category.LAUNCHER" />
            </intent-filter>
        </activity>

        <activity android:name=".SettingsActivity" />

    </application>
</manifest>
questions.explanation: Every Activity must be declared in AndroidManifest.xml. Even though the class compiles, the Android system doesn't know about it without a manifest entry. Add <activity android:name=".SettingsActivity" /> inside <application>.
hints: All Android components must be declared somewhere the system can find them
Check what's missing between the application tags
